figures.caption: Fig. 1. In cognitive neuroscience, multiple candidate regions compete for limited representation, namely biased competition. In Ref-AVS, the competition is under the joint influence of visual context and the task-driven goal from the referring expression.
TABLE I PERFORMANCE COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS ON THE REF-AVS BENCHMARK. ∗ DENOTES METHODS ADAPTED TO THE TRI-MODAL SETTING. THE MIX MEANS THE AVERAGE VALUE OF SEEN AND UNSEEN SPLITS.
Fig. 6. Attention visualization for the biased competition ablation. Without biased competition, attention is more easily drawn to the flute as a competing distractor.
Fig. 8. Failure cases of our proposed PRIMED. Please zoom in to see more details. Speaker icons indicate the sounding objects.

Fig. 2. Overview of PRIMED. Multimodal features extracted from encoders, together with distilled visual tokens, are fused through stage-wise CBCF modules to generate embeddings for the mask decoder. The modality prior from MPD modulates the highest-level CBCF module. During training, the SASA loss further enforces semantic discrimination between foreground and background regions.
Fig. 4. Prompts used for the label annotation audit.
Fig. 3. Prompts used for the label annotation of Modality Prior Decoder.
Fig. 7. Visualization results of referred objects in the Ref-AVS benchmark. Please zoom in to see more details. Speaker icons indicate the sounding objects.
